EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Mid Atlantic and was accompanied by showers and thunderstorms that produced gusty winds. A strong jet of 50 knot winds was located at 850 mb or about a mile off the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down at 6:36 pm EDT on the Westwood Shopping Plaza ripping off roofing materials and ventilation units.  It traveled northeast through a residential area uprooting and topping trees and causing roofing damage.  The tornado passed just west of the Mercer Elementary School and adjacent Police Barracks ripping the roof off of an apartment building along Cowan Blvd and throwing it across the street.  A law enforcement officer saw the tornado cross Cowan Blvd and then dissipate before it reached Mary Washington Hospital at 6:37 PM.

Although to some the damage appeared to have been caused by a tornado, it was determined that the damage was a result of strong thunderstorm straight-line winds, not a tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Buckingham County Emergency Management Director reported that over two dozen trees were blown down across the central and eastern part of the county from just west-southwest and northwest of Dillwyn to just south-southeast of Gold Hill.  There were also several incidents of structural damage as well.  Several trees were down along Buffalo Road northwest of Dillwyn and several trees were also down between Sprouses Corner and the Cumberland county line between Dillwyn and Gold Hill.  Two trees were down on Fanny White Road near the Buckingham County High School.  Other individual trees were down on Andersonville Road southwest of Dillwyn, near Sprouses Corner west of Dillwyn, and Gravel Hill Road south-southeast of Gold Hill.  A home on Main Street in Dillwyn sustained at least $50,000 in damages from downed trees on the structure.  Another home on Mitchell Street in Dillwyn sustained $5,000 in damages.  Two barns on Rosney Road in Dillwyn were destroyed with an estimated $30,000 in damages.  In addition to these damages, a sign and metal pole were damaged just west-southwest of Dillwyn, air conditioner units were blown off a building on the northwest side of Dillwyn, and the roof was blown off a trailer home just south-southeast of Gold Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As had been typical through much of the later half of the winter season across the eastern U.S., unseasonably cold air was in place across the region once again.  Morning low temperatures on this day were in the 20s across western Virginia with 30s in the Piedmont.  Meanwhile, a strong upper-level low pressure area in the southern stream was moving toward the region from the Mid-South.  A cold front was stalled along the Georgia and South Carolina Coast with a wave of surface low pressure tracking northeastward along the front near the Georgia Coast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, full-latitude upper trough was sweeping from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S. during this period.  As with the event exactly one week before, a warm, moist air mass was present across the region in advance of this front.  Once again, a strong line of thunderstorms, or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) formed in advance of the cold front associated with the upper low, moving through the region during peak heating time, namely afternoon and early evening.  Some discrete strong cells were also noted in advance of the main thunderstorm line, but many of these managed to remain just east or south of the Blacksburg County Warning Area (CWA).  As with the event on April 12th, the Piedmont region of Southside Virginia and north central North Carolina was the hardest hit.  Dozens to hundreds of trees were blown down by strong thunderstorm winds gusts from Patrick county on the west end to Buckingham county on the east end.  Considerable structural damage was noted across Buckingham county in particular, as well as parts of Appomattox county, Bedford county, and the City of Danville, mostly from downed trees on homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that one tree was blown down onto a home on Frazier Road just north of Alta Vista and another tree was down along Lynch Mill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, full-latitude upper trough was sweeping from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S. during this period.  As with the event exactly one week before, a warm, moist air mass was present across the region in advance of this front.  Once again, a strong line of thunderstorms, or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) formed in advance of the cold front associated with the upper low, moving through the region during peak heating time, namely afternoon and early evening.  Some discrete strong cells were also noted in advance of the main thunderstorm line, but many of these managed to remain just east or south of the Blacksburg County Warning Area (CWA).  As with the event on April 12th, the Piedmont region of Southside Virginia and north central North Carolina was the hardest hit.  Dozens to hundreds of trees were blown down by strong thunderstorm winds gusts from Patrick county on the west end to Buckingham county on the east end.  Considerable structural damage was noted across Buckingham county in particular, as well as parts of Appomattox county, Bedford county, and the City of Danville, mostly from downed trees on homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest Virginia Broadcast Media reported that from 30 to 40 trees were blown down approximately five miles northwest of Evington.  The Bedford County 911 Center reported that another tree was down near the intersection of Virginia Route 24 and New London Road., also just northwest of Evington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With flood waters from heavy rainfall during the afternoon and evening of January 30th rapidly running off from higher terrain and collecting in area river basins, many area rivers exceeded flood stage on January 31st. River flooding was observed at many points along the Dan, James and New Rivers, as well as numerous small rivers and creeks across southwest and central Virginia. Flood waters in hillier terrain receded more quickly, however rivers across the Piedmont region of Virginia remained in flood into the first days of February as they absorbed the collected waters from rainfall occurring in the mountains to the west. Aside from flooded roads adjacent to some of the more swollen rivers, river flooding across the area was minimized due to very low river levels as a result of minor to moderately dry conditions across the region during the autumn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low water bridge on Epperly Lane was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked from northern Georgia to the North Carolina coast.  Warm air in the low levels allowed precipitation to fall initially as rain, however cold air wrapping around the west side of the low caused rain to change over to a brief period of intense sleet, before changing over to snow across the mountains during the afternoon, and across the piedmonts during the evening.  Snowfall rates were at times 2 to 3 inches per hour for some locations.  Snowfall came to an end quickly from west to east during late evening as drier air aloft moved into the area.  Heaviest snowfall accumulations were observed in the mountains due to the longer residence time of colder air, however the piedmont region experienced less snowfall due to warmer air lingering across the area longer, resulting in only a brief but intense burst of snowfall just as the system was exiting the area.||The following is a list of total snowfall reports, in inches, from across Virginia.|| - EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked from northern Georgia to the North Carolina coast.  Warm air in the low levels allowed precipitation to fall initially as rain, however cold air wrapping around the west side of the low caused rain to change over to a brief period of intense sleet, before changing over to snow across the mountains during the afternoon, and across the piedmonts during the evening.  Snowfall rates were at times 2 to 3 inches per hour for some locations.  Snowfall came to an end quickly from west to east during late evening as drier air aloft moved into the area.  Heaviest snowfall accumulations were observed in the mountains due to the longer residence time of colder air, however the piedmont region experienced less snowfall due to warmer air lingering across the area longer, resulting in only a brief but intense burst of snowfall just as the system was exiting the area.||The following is a list of total snowfall reports, in inches, from across Virginia.|| - EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving off the Mid Atlantic Coast produced between one and five inches of snow across portions of central and eastern Virginia Thursday morning, January 24th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was wedged down the east coast and surface temperatures fell below freezing. Low pressure developed across the gulf coast states and moved across the Ohio Valley. Snow began but changed over to sleet and freezing rain as warmer temperatures aloft overran the cold air at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of around a tenth of an inch fell at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled to the south of the Mid Atlantic region and a weak disturbance  moved along it. Northerly flow caused temperatures to get below freezing west of the Blue Ridge and Northern Maryland. Moisture and warmer air aloft streamed in from the Atlantic and freezing rain formed where temperatures were below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice reports of less than a tenth inch were reported at surrounding observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving off the Mid Atlantic Coast produced between one and three inches of snow across portions of central and eastern Virginia Friday afternoon into Friday evening, January 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between one and two inches across the county. Wallops Island reported 2.0 inches of snow. Chincoteague reported 1.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved through the eastern U.S. Surface low pressure formed near the Carolinas and tracked eastward towards the Atlantic Coast. Moisture ahead of this system aided in snow development as well as instability. Areas in central Virginia saw the most snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations averaged 7 inches in the county. Public reported this total at Wintergreen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was wedged down the east coast and surface temperatures fell below freezing. Low pressure developed across the gulf coast states and moved across the Ohio Valley. Snow began but changed over to sleet and freezing rain as warmer temperatures aloft overran the cold air at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation around a quarter inch were measured surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked from northern Georgia to the North Carolina coast.  Warm air in the low levels allowed precipitation to fall initially as rain, however cold air wrapping around the west side of the low caused rain to change over to a brief period of intense sleet, before changing over to snow across the mountains during the afternoon, and across the piedmonts during the evening.  Snowfall rates were at times 2 to 3 inches per hour for some locations.  Snowfall came to an end quickly from west to east during late evening as drier air aloft moved into the area.  Heaviest snowfall accumulations were observed in the mountains due to the longer residence time of colder air, however the piedmont region experienced less snowfall due to warmer air lingering across the area longer, resulting in only a brief but intense burst of snowfall just as the system was exiting the area.||The following is a list of total snowfall reports, in inches, from across Virginia.|| - EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked from northern Georgia to the North Carolina coast.  Warm air in the low levels allowed precipitation to fall initially as rain, however cold air wrapping around the west side of the low caused rain to change over to a brief period of intense sleet, before changing over to snow across the mountains during the afternoon, and across the piedmonts during the evening.  Snowfall rates were at times 2 to 3 inches per hour for some locations.  Snowfall came to an end quickly from west to east during late evening as drier air aloft moved into the area.  Heaviest snowfall accumulations were observed in the mountains due to the longer residence time of colder air, however the piedmont region experienced less snowfall due to warmer air lingering across the area longer, resulting in only a brief but intense burst of snowfall just as the system was exiting the area.||The following is a list of total snowfall reports, in inches, from across Virginia.|| - EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked from northern Georgia to the North Carolina coast.  Warm air in the low levels allowed precipitation to fall initially as rain, however cold air wrapping around the west side of the low caused rain to change over to a brief period of intense sleet, before changing over to snow across the mountains during the afternoon, and across the piedmonts during the evening.  Snowfall rates were at times 2 to 3 inches per hour for some locations.  Snowfall came to an end quickly from west to east during late evening as drier air aloft moved into the area.  Heaviest snowfall accumulations were observed in the mountains due to the longer residence time of colder air, however the piedmont region experienced less snowfall due to warmer air lingering across the area longer, resulting in only a brief but intense burst of snowfall just as the system was exiting the area.||The following is a list of total snowfall reports, in inches, from across Virginia.|| - EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With flood waters from heavy rainfall during the afternoon and evening of January 30th rapidly running off from higher terrain and collecting in area river basins, many area rivers exceeded flood stage on January 31st. River flooding was observed at many points along the Dan, James and New Rivers, as well as numerous small rivers and creeks across southwest and central Virginia. Flood waters in hillier terrain receded more quickly, however rivers across the Piedmont region of Virginia remained in flood into the first days of February as they absorbed the collected waters from rainfall occurring in the mountains to the west. Aside from flooded roads adjacent to some of the more swollen rivers, river flooding across the area was minimized due to very low river levels as a result of minor to moderately dry conditions across the region during the autumn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 100 cars were partially or completely submerged at the Dedmon Center parking lot at Radford University when the New River overflowed its banks. Flooding on the New River was aggravated by water releases at Claytor Lake Dam which took place after midnight on January 31st. It is estimated that the New River near the Dedmon Center rose nine feet in one hour between 2:30 AM and 3:30 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong lifting of a moist air mass in the presence of an upper level low pressure system resulted in a heavy wet snowfall event during the period from around noon est until early evening. Atmospheric dynamics were so intense at times, that lightning was generated; i.e.  thundersnow. Much of the snow accumulated across the region from Central East Tennessee northeast through Southwest Virginia, generally north of Interstate 40 and east of Interstate 75. The upper level low moved east from Northern to Northeast Georgia generating 3 to 5 inches of snow across the Great Valley of Central East Tennessee northeast to the Tri-Cities area. Much greater snowfall amounts occurred across Southwest Virginia with enhancement in the higher terrain in this region as well as over the mountains of Northeast Tennessee. In these areas, snowfall amounts ranged from 6 inches to as much as a foot in the highest elevations across Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was reported at Pennington Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure over southern New England combined with low pressure moving northeast across the mid atlantic region produced around one tenth of an inch or less of ice from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced around 0.10 inch or less of ice accumulation. This resulted in some slick roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked from northern Georgia to the North Carolina coast.  Warm air in the low levels allowed precipitation to fall initially as rain, however cold air wrapping around the west side of the low caused rain to change over to a brief period of intense sleet, before changing over to snow across the mountains during the afternoon, and across the piedmonts during the evening.  Snowfall rates were at times 2 to 3 inches per hour for some locations.  Snowfall came to an end quickly from west to east during late evening as drier air aloft moved into the area.  Heaviest snowfall accumulations were observed in the mountains due to the longer residence time of colder air, however the piedmont region experienced less snowfall due to warmer air lingering across the area longer, resulting in only a brief but intense burst of snowfall just as the system was exiting the area.||The following is a list of total snowfall reports, in inches, from across Virginia.|| - EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts across Appomattox County ranged from 4.6 inches in Appomattox to 1.5 inches at 9 E Lynchburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong lifting of a moist air mass in the presence of an upper level low pressure system resulted in a heavy wet snowfall event during the period from around noon est until early evening. Atmospheric dynamics were so intense at times, that lightning was generated; i.e.  thundersnow. Much of the snow accumulated across the region from Central East Tennessee northeast through Southwest Virginia, generally north of Interstate 40 and east of Interstate 75. The upper level low moved east from Northern to Northeast Georgia generating 3 to 5 inches of snow across the Great Valley of Central East Tennessee northeast to the Tri-Cities area. Much greater snowfall amounts occurred across Southwest Virginia with enhancement in the higher terrain in this region as well as over the mountains of Northeast Tennessee. In these areas, snowfall amounts ranged from 6 inches to as much as a foot in the highest elevations across Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow was reported at Lebanon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled to the south of the Mid Atlantic region and a weak disturbance  moved along it. Northerly flow caused temperatures to get below freezing west of the Blue Ridge and Northern Maryland. Moisture and warmer air aloft streamed in from the Atlantic and freezing rain formed where temperatures were below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice reports of less than a tenth inch were reported at surrounding observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With flood waters from heavy rainfall during the afternoon and evening of January 30th rapidly running off from higher terrain and collecting in area river basins, many area rivers exceeded flood stage on January 31st. River flooding was observed at many points along the Dan, James and New Rivers, as well as numerous small rivers and creeks across southwest and central Virginia. Flood waters in hillier terrain receded more quickly, however rivers across the Piedmont region of Virginia remained in flood into the first days of February as they absorbed the collected waters from rainfall occurring in the mountains to the west. Aside from flooded roads adjacent to some of the more swollen rivers, river flooding across the area was minimized due to very low river levels as a result of minor to moderately dry conditions across the region during the autumn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15 foot by 5 foot sink hole was observed on Mountain View Road, which was caused by heavy rainfall in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of around an inch were reported at Reagan National during the morning rush hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between three and four inches across the county. Meherrin and Victoria reported 4.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving off the North Carolina Coast produced between three and six inches of snow across portions of central and south central Virginia from Thursday afternoon into Thursday night, January 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between three and four inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was located across southwest Virginia.  In advance of this frontal boundary was an extremely moist air mass with precipitable water values around 2.0 inches that had persisted across the region much of the later half of July.  In addition, a persistent upper-level trough was noted across the eastern U.S. with a strong subtropical ridge in the western U.S.  The combination of these factors contributed to yet another afternoon/evening of pulse thunderstorms that persisted well after midnight in areas of local wind convergence.  Rainfall rates were excessive, and exceeded four inches in a four hour or less period across parts of Halifax county causing some small creeks and streams to flow out of their banks.  This event continued into the early morning hours of August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Halifax County 911 Center reported that Poplar Creek near the intersection of Summit Drive and Railroad Street in South Boston was out of its banks causing Summit Drive in this area to be closed.  The water was at least three inches deep flowing over the road. Damages were confined to minor debris removal.  This event continued into the early morning hours of August 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the region overnight. Prior to the frontal passage, high pressure over the region led to fog development in the Potomac Highlands and sheltered valleys west of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities around a quarter of a mile were measured at Luray Airport and by satellite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough entered the Mid Atlantic in the afternoon and showers and thunderstorms form along a lee trough just east of the Appalachians. Heating during the day led to instability to fuel thunderstorms and gusty winds occurred in Central VA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were trees down blocking the roadway at the 1000 block of Secretarys Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry Canadian high pressure, centered over western Ohio to start the day, made its way across the mid Atlantic, resulting in clear skies, very light northeasterly winds and afternoon temperatures roughly 5 to 8 degrees below normal for mid-September for much of the region.  The dust devil is thought to be the result of differential heating at the interface between different surface types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust devil lifted several large, tied-down canopy tents into the air. The canopies then dropped down onto a crowd of people. There were six minor injuries, with one person taken to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region and a surface trough was located near the I-95 corridor. Southerly flow ahead of the cold front brought warm and humid conditions to the area. This led to instability and showers and thunderstorms formed near the pressure trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were trees down on Milltown Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low off the NC coast along with surface high pressure over New England lead to an extended period of strong onshore flow resulting in coastal flooding across the Chesapeake Bay and James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal anomalies of 2.5 feet produced moderate flooding along the western coast of the Chesapeake Bay. Windmill Point recorded a maximum storm tide of 3.8 feet mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was located across the region resulting in colder than normal temperatures. During the evening, calm and clear conditions led to temperatures to fall due to radiational cooling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of 32 degrees or less were measured at surrounding observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the region triggered widespread rain showers ahead of the boundary, with little to no lightning observed.  Early morning showers and cloud cover strictly limited heating, resulting in low CAPE values.  The main line of convection passed across the mountains during early afternoon, triggering winds in the 25 to 35 mph range per weather spotter reports.  However, as the line passed east of the Blue Ridge, it encountered a stronger low level jet and higher shear and helicity, which helped the convection to rapidly intensify.  Strong wind reports remained spotty along the main line, with isolated reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down on Leesville Road just north of the Town Fork Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across central and eastern Virginia. Some storms produced strong winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in the vicinity of Route 360 and Lee Davis Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was located across the Mid Atlantic and clear and calm conditions led to temperatures to fall below freezing.  This ended the growing season in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of 32 degrees or less were reported at surrounding mesonets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved through the Mid West and warm moist air was in place across the Mid Atlantic. Showers and thunderstorms crossed the Potomac Highlands in the morning and across the I-95 corridor by early afternoon. Thunderstorms were capable of damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several HVAC units toppled over on the roof of Apple Blossom Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was located across the Mid Atlantic and clear skies, light winds and antecedent conditions led to dense fog formation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities of a quarter mile or less were measured at Shenandoah Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across the Mid Atlantic and low pressure formed and moved along it. Southerly flow advected gulf moisture into the region and showers and thunderstorms easily formed. Heavy rain, hail, damaging winds and tornadoes occurred throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After review of radar observations and a ground survey, The National Weather Service has confirmed a EF-0 tornado touched down in Southeastern King George County on the afternoon of Thursday, June 13, 2013.  ||The tornado touched down in Port Conway, where tree damage occurred just east of US 301.  The tornado then traveled east-northeast across southeastern King George County causing intermittent tree damage.  The most significant damage occurred Northeast of Rollings Fork near the Westmorland County border where three 20-30 inch diameter Poplar trees were blown over and numerous small trees were topped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow moving cold front extended through the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia into southwest Virginia.  A very moist and unstable air mass persisted in advance of the front across the Virginia Piedmont, where precipitable water values had remained in excess of 2.0 inches for several days.  While the main concern throughout the period had been and continued to be localized significant flash flooding, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening just in advance of the boundary from near Washington D. C. to near Charlottesville, then backbuilded into Buckingham county in the far northeast part of the Blacksburg WFO CWA.  These thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Buckingham County Sheriff's Office reported that one tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds just west of New Canton on Bridgeport Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that one tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds on Virginia Route 20 just north-northeast of Centenary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across southern West Virginia into central Virginia.  A broad upper-level low persisted across the northeast United States and southeast Canada while a broad subtropical ridge remained in place across the southern United States.  Very moist tropical air with precipitable water values near 2.0 inches persisted across southern Virginia and northern North Carolina.  With daytime heating, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon from near Martinsville, Virginia southwestward into western North Carolina producing damaging winds gusts. While this line of thunderstorms moved south out of Virginia, additional more isolated thunderstorms developed back to the northwest with time during the mid-afternoon.  One of these developed across Halifax county causing considerable damage at the Lowes in South Boston, VA and blowing other trees down across the county as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Halifax County 911 Center reported that a tree limb blown down by thunderstorm winds took down a power line along Halifax Road near the Schewel Furniture Store.

This event began during the last hour of the day on July 31, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Halifax County 911 Center reported that Poplar Creek, near the intersection of Summit Drive and Railroad Street in South Boston, was out of its banks causing Summit Drive in this area to be closed.  The water was at least three inches deep flowing over the road. Damages were confined to minor debris removal.  This event began at 1110 pm EST on July 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front to a warm front remain draped across southern Virginia and southern West Virginia.  This boundary combined with a persistent moist, unstable air mass across the region served as a focusing mechanism for scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorm development across the region once again.  As had been the case much of the summer of 2013, heavy rain and flash flooding were once again the main concerns.  Thunderstorms across southwest Virginia during the evening prompted several flash flood warnings netting several flash flood events as rainfall of two to four inches fell in flash flood prone areas within a couple of hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carroll County 911 Center reported that several inches of water were flowing over Ivanhoe Road approximately five miles north of Fries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was over saturated Mid-Atlantic region and calm winds and clear skies led to dense fog development in parts of the Virginia Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities of less than a quarter mile were measured at Luray Airport.

A broad upper-level low persisted across the northeast United States and southeast Canada while a broad subtropical ridge remained in place across the southern United States. With daytime heating, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon from near Martinsville, Virginia southwestward into western North Carolina producing damaging winds gusts. While this line of thunderstorms moved south out of Virginia, additional more isolated thunderstorms developed back to the northwest with time during the mid-afternoon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Halifax County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds along U.S. Route 360 just north of its intersection with Virginia Route 613.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well south of the prefrontal convection, a small cluster of showers and thunderstorms became anchored over the mountains of southern Buchanan County during the early evening on Labor Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain estimates of 1.5 to 2 inches in an hour and a storm total of 2 to 2.5 inches in 3 hours fell mainly southwest of Vansant toward Leemaster and Prater.||Small streams, such as Trace Fork Branch and War Fork, flooded and damaged roads.  Water was around a mobile home along Route 83.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level trough persisted just to the west of the area.  A warm, humid air mass remained in place across the region along with numerous outflow boundaries from earlier convection.  A near solid line of thunderstorms was moving into southern West Virginia and far southwest Virginia during the early evening.  The tail end of this line produced a severe thunderstorm with damaging wind gusts in Tazewell county before it weakened as it moved further eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tazewell County Sheriff's Office reported that two large tree limbs were blown off trees by thunderstorm winds on Front Street in downtown Richlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front to a warm front remain draped across southern Virginia and southern West Virginia.  This boundary combined with a persistent moist, unstable air mass across the region served as a focusing mechanism for scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorm development across the region once again.  As had been the case much of the summer of 2013, heavy rain and flash flooding were once again the main concerns.  Thunderstorms across southwest Virginia during the evening prompted several flash flood warnings netting several flash flood events as rainfall of two to four inches fell in flash flood prone areas within a couple of hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and the Virginia Department of Highways reported that several creeks in the Ceres area were out of their banks causing the flooding of several roads and adjacent areas.  Virginia Route 626 was flooded from Page Branch where a parked vehicle was partially submerged.  Heninger Branch Creek on the south side of Ceres was also out of its banks rendering Stowers Hill Road impassable.  Finally, Birch Grove Road on the southeast side of Ceres was also flooded.  A spotter near Ceres recorded 3.25 inches of rain in less than an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad west-northwest flow aloft was present across the region on the northeast side of a strong subtropical upper ridge centered over the southern plains.  Weak disturbances were embedded within the flow aloft.  Although the air mass across the region was not as moist as it had been in previous days around the 1st of the month, precipitable water values across the Piedmont were still near 2.0 inches.  Only isolated thunderstorms were noted with this event, but given the very saturated conditions from a persistent very wet summer, strong, slow moving thunderstorms across the urban Roanoke area resulted in flood problems once again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Roanoke 911 Center reported flooding at the intersection of King Street and Berkley Street.  Doppler Radar estimated rainfall indicated that 1.5 to 2.0 inches of rain fell in the area within about two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist air mass persisted across the region.  Precipitable water values remained near 2.0 inches.  A frontal boundary was stalled just to the west of the area.  Just as was the case on the 8th, scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours.  On this day, one area of fairly concentrated thunderstorms developed along the Blue Ridge Mountains across Patrick county and persisted in that area for a couple of hours during the evening.  Rainfall amounts near four inches were observed near Stuart and Critz, which resulted in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that flash flooding at the intersection of Spring Road and Pleasant View Drive, caused by overflowing Laurel Branch Creek, resulted in a vehicle being inundated which subsequently stalled out.  Several large tree logs, approximately 10 to 15 feet long, stored in the vicinity, were washed about 300 yards downstream from their original location.  Radar-estimated rainfall from the area was three to four inches in less than two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist air mass persisted across the region.  Precipitable water values remained near 2.0 inches.  A frontal boundary was stalled just to the west of the area.  Just as was the case on the 8th, scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours.  On this day, one area of fairly concentrated thunderstorms developed along the Blue Ridge Mountains across Patrick county and persisted in that area for a couple of hours during the evening.  Rainfall amounts near four inches were observed near Stuart and Critz, which resulted in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports sent to the Blacksburg, Virginia Facebook and Twitter Pages indicated that both Anglin Branch Creek and Rich Creek were overflowing as a result of heavy rainfall in the area resulting in flash flooding to adjacent areas.  Martin Farm Road was flooded at the Anglin Branch Creek bridge with water flowing over the road.  Dr. Kay Lane near the Animal Clinic was flooded by Rich Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very weak upper trough persisted across the region on the east side of a strong subtropical ridge that continued to expand through the central and southeastern states.  A very weak diffuse cold front lingered through the region, but was rapidly washing out.  The air mass across the region had become increasingly moist and unstable.  While atmospheric dynamics were weak, sufficient lift, moisture, and instability were present for scattered pop-up pulse thunderstorms to develop across the region, especially Southside Virginia and north central North Carolina.  A few of these became severe producing damaging wind gusts in North Carolina.  While none of the thunderstorms on the Virginia side were severe, one in Patrick county produced penny-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively strong cold front, for August, was moving southward through the area as a large area of Canadian High Pressure moved southward through the Great Lakes.  |Unstable northwest flow aloft remained over the area on the east side of a strong subtropical ridge located across the Midwest.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon, especially across the Piedmont, along the southward moving west-east oriented frontal boundary.  A few of these became strong to severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Henry County Sheriff's Office reported that a large tree limb and a couple of power lines were blown down by thunderstorm winds along Carver Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper trough was deepening across the central U.S.  A cold front was approaching southwest Virginia from the west.  Very moist tropical air was present across the region once again as this event unfolded, with precipitable water values once again near or in excess of 2.0 inches across the area.  Thunderstorms developed quickly during the afternoon across southwest Virginia, especially across Giles and Wythe counties.  Flash flooding was widespread across much of Giles county, including the cities of Pembroke, Pearisburg, and Rich Creek.  The flash flooding was quite significant in Pembroke where a number of homes and businesses sustained damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WDBJ7 Television of Roanoke, Virginia and WVVA Television of Bluefield, WV reported that significant flash flooding affected the community of Pembroke in northeastern Giles county.  Nearly three inches of rain fell in an hour causing creeks, streams, and drainage ditches in the area to quickly flash flood the town, which is in a relatively low elevation area compared to surrounding higher terrain and mountains (e.g., Pembroke's elevation is about 1600 feet, while Mountain Lake just a few miles to the east is over 4000 feet and Peters mountain just to the north is near 3500 feet elevation).  Although flooding was reported along Doe Creek, the most significant flash flooding was the result of a clogged drainage ditch that runs under U.S. Highway 460 just east of downtown Pembroke.  U.S. Highway 460 was completely closed during the event as mud, rocks, gravel, tree limbs, roots, and other debris washed out over the road.  A number of homes and businesses in the area suffered extensive damage from the flood waters and resultant mud and debris that damaged the structures.  Several vehicles were also flooded in the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable northwest flow aloft persisted across the region thanks to an equally persistent strong subtropical ridge across the central U.S.  A strengthening upper level storm system was diving southeast from the Great Lakes.  The air mass across the region was unseasonably warm for late August, and as a result was quite unstable.  These factors combined to produce  a few|severe thunderstorms.  The initial thunderstorms developed across far southwest Virginia and southeast West Virginia during the early to mid-afternoon as a result of outflow boundaries from overnight thunderstorms that moved through Kentucky, Tennessee and western West Virginia.  The thunderstorms propagated and developed eastward through the evening, impacting much of the Blacksburg, VA County Warning Area (CWA).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained storm spotter reported quarter-sized hail approximately six miles west of Moneta on the north side of Smith Mountain Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: ABC13 Television of Lynchburg, Danville, Roanoke, reported that thunderstorm winds caused extensive damage at the Lowes in South Boston, Virginia.  Within just a few minutes, nine storage sheds located in the front parking lot for sale demonstration, were destroyed by the strong straight-line thunderstorm winds.  Some of the buildings were wood and some metal and were as large as 8 feet by 12 feet.  No other property was damaged and no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed in a moist and moderately to strongly unstable air mass situated on the periphery of a large Upper Level High Pressure System over the Ohio Valley. The storms produced mostly wind damage. However, training convective cells produced some major flash flooding across the Tri-Cities area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding observed across Gate City and Speers Ferry with some road closures.

An upper level low approached the region and a upper level jet added to enhanced lift in the region.  Heavy rain caused a lot of flash flooding with amounts up to seven inches in less than 6 hours recorded near Washington Dulles Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Taylors Gap Road was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced widespread wind damage and embedded large hail across much of central and eastern Virginia. A child was killed by a falling tree in Richmond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds blew over a billboard near exit 4 along Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the region in the form of a squall line of storms. Winds of 60 to 70 mph accompanied this squall line, resulting a widespread wind damage. Large hail also accompanied some of the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds from a squall line brought trees and power lines down from Thomasson Mill Road southeast of Stewartsville through Smith Mountain Lake and to Planters Drive near Huddleston. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and storms developed during the afternoon hours. A couple of these increased to severe levels and produced large hail and damaging winds. Also, heavy rains from one storm caused some minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from the size of dimes to quarters fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening due to strong instability associated with an approaching upper level trough and the associated cold front.

A weak cold front moved through the region and stalled over the area.  Convergence along the boundary led to heavy showers.  A few breaks in the clouds led to an increase in instability and thunderstorms that produced heavy rain in activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: University Ave at Business US-29 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening due to strong instability associated with an approaching upper level trough and the associated cold front.

A cold front stalled across the Mid Atlantic and low pressure formed and moved along it. Southerly flow advected gulf moisture into the region and showers and thunderstorms easily formed. Heavy rain, hail, damaging winds and tornadoes occurred throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was widespread tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was anchored off shore while low pressure spun over the Great Lakes. A shortwave trough moved over the region initiating showers and thunderstorms in vicinity of a surface trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few trees and large branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed during the afternoon heating period in a moisture laden atmosphere. Some of these storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a few trees down in the Hardy area along Wysong Mill Road and Deepwoods Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front over the Eastern Virginia Piedmont. Strong gusty winds and a tornado were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed along River Road (Highway 6).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary moved northward and stalled over the Mid-Atlantic/I-95 corridor overnight.  An upper level low approached the region and a upper level jet added to enhanced lift in the region.  Heavy rain caused a lot of flash flooding with amounts up to seven inches in less than 6 hours recorded near Washington Dulles Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A road was washed out in the Branch Mountain area off Patrick Henry Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level trough was moving eastward and across most of the eastern U.S. with a surface low pressure center across the eastern Great Lakes.  A cold front moved through the Mid Atlantic with dew points in the low 60s ahead of frontal passage.  A strong low level jet on the eastern side of trough aided in strong winds reaching the surface.  Flood and flash flood also occurred along or just ahead of the frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several branches about three inches in diameter knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the region triggered widespread rain showers ahead of the boundary, with little to no lightning observed.  Early morning showers and cloud cover strictly limited heating, resulting in low CAPE values.  The main line of convection passed across the mountains during early afternoon, triggering winds in the 25 to 35 mph range per weather spotter reports.  However, as the line passed east of the Blue Ridge, it encountered a stronger low level jet and higher shear and helicity, which helped the convection to rapidly intensify.  Strong wind reports remained spotty along the main line, with isolated reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the intersection of Turners Creek Road and Five Mile Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree was downed along Route 740 (Turkey Egg Road).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked north of the Mid Atlantic. Easterly flow transported moisture into the region and surface convergence led to storms that produced heavy rainfall, large hail and damaging winds.
A squall line produced widespread wind damage and embedded large hail across much of central and eastern Virginia. A child was killed by a falling tree in Richmond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 60 mph produced widespread tree damage across the county. The most significant damage occurred at Lowe's in Riverdale where several storage sheds were destroyed. A large tree was blocking the off-ramp at exit 3B on Interstate-295. Numerous trees were reported down along Prince George Boulevard as well as throughout Disputanta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced widespread wind damage and embedded large hail across much of central and eastern Virginia. A child was killed by a falling tree in Richmond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large downed tree blocked Bennetts Pasture Road near US-17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms well in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds across portions of central, south central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon convection again bubbled up over southwest Virginia in evening hours of the 12th with very heavy rains over the City of Galax, Grayson and Carroll counties where 2 to 3 inch rains fell. Several flooding reports were received. Several gauges in the Galax area had over 2 inches for the period ending 0700 EDT on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Highway 94 was washed out resulting in the road closure.
